title: SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic: The S&P 500 Inclusion Question and Investment Consequences!
gist: Damodaran argues that opposition to including SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic in the S&P 500 is driven by hidden agendas (bruised active managers, paternalistic experts, populist politicians) rather than sound reasoning, and that empirical evidence shows the index-inclusion price bump has faded to near-nothing, so these firms will not be materially helped or hurt by the one-year wait S&P has imposed on them.

insights.claim: Opposition to including SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic in the S&P 500 comes mainly from three groups: active investors bruised by two decades of losing to passive investing, investing experts/academics claiming to protect retail investors, and politicians attacking billionaires via pension-fund exposure.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should discount index-exclusion arguments as self-interested noise rather than signal about company quality or risk.
insights.quote: I am skeptical, since I see a combination of hidden agendas and misguided views about investing behind each of the three groups that are most vehemently against inclusions.
insights.locator: Over the last few weeks, attention has

insights.claim: Active fund managers who have lost the passive-versus-active battle for two decades frame S&P 500 inclusion of these mega-cap money-losers as a new risk that justifies paying professional managers to avoid it.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Watch for asset managers marketing new SpaceX/OpenAI/Anthropic-exclusion or active products as a hedge product line.
insights.locator: Over the last few weeks, attention has

insights.claim: Politicians framing S&P 500 inclusion of these companies as rewarding billionaires (through exposure in government pension funds) are, in Damodaran's view, not credible sources of investment wisdom.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Populist political pressure on index rules is a tail risk to monitor but not a fundamentals-based reason to avoid these names.
insights.locator: Over the last few weeks, attention has

insights.claim: S&P 500 inclusion requires a company to have been listed at least a year, meet free-float liquidity standards, and post positive profits in the four quarters leading into listing.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: This one-year listing rule is the exact mechanism that delays SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic's index eligibility regardless of size.

insights.claim: Of the three common index-weighting schemes (equal-weight, price-weight, market-cap-weight), price-weighting (used by the Dow 30) has no redeeming quality because it measures little of consequence and breaks down after stock splits.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors comparing indices should recognize price-weighted benchmarks like the Dow 30 as a weaker gauge of true market performance than cap-weighted alternatives.
insights.quote: I cannot think of a single redeeming quality to price weighting, since it measures very little of consequence, and suffers from breakdowns, right after stock splits.

insights.claim: Because the S&P 500 weights by free-float market cap, Meta trades below its true weight since Zuckerberg's Class B voting shares aren't counted, and Walmart is similarly under-weighted due to family-controlled holdings treated as non-traded.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Free-float adjustments mean reported index weights understate the economic size of founder/family-controlled mega-caps like Meta and Walmart.

insights.claim: As of June 15, 2026, the S&P 500 closed at 7554.29 with cumulative float-adjusted market capitalization of $63,498.44 billion across its 500 constituents, implying an index-unit conversion factor of 0.1190.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete, dated snapshot of aggregate S&P 500 market cap useful for sizing how much a trillion-dollar addition would move index composition.

insights.claim: In 2025 the S&P 500's aggregated dollar dividends totaled $664.90 billion, which converts to an index dividend of 79.12 when multiplied by the 0.1190 index-unit factor.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Establishes the baseline index dividend level against which the dilutive effect of adding non-dividend-paying mega-caps can be measured.

insights.claim: When a trillion-dollar company replaces a billion-dollar company in the S&P 500, the index level itself doesn't change at the moment of replacement, but index units adjust and the index's forward fundamentals shift, bringing in more risk, a near-term earnings hit, and potentially higher long-term growth.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Adding SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic won't move the S&P 500's headline level on day one, but will structurally raise the index's risk profile and depress near-term aggregate earnings going forward.

insights.claim: The S&P 500 represents more than 80% of the total market capitalization of the roughly 6,000 publicly traded US companies despite including only 500 names, which is why it has become the default proxy for aggregate US equity performance.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Explains why S&P 500 inclusion carries outsized symbolic weight even though its practical price effect has diminished.

insights.claim: Over the last twenty-five years, active large-cap fund managers beat the S&P 500 in only three years, and barely so, with staggering underperformance in the rest.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of active management's failure against its most-referenced benchmark, reinforcing the case for indexing.

insights.claim: S&P created SPIVA (and Morningstar a similar tool) to compare fund managers against style-matched index funds (e.g., small-cap value managers versus small-cap value index funds), breaking the long-standing excuse that managers were being judged against the wrong benchmark.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Removes the risk-adjustment escape hatch active managers used for decades, making underperformance claims far harder to dispute.

insights.claim: Using SPIVA's style-matched methodology, there is not a single fund group in any investment style that has outperformed its respective index over the past ten years, in the US or in other geographies.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Undercuts the common claim that active management works better in less-efficient non-US markets.
insights.quote: the SPIVA results are devastating and damning for any claim of active investing superiority

insights.claim: Private equity and hedge funds, once considered smarter-than-market vehicles that pulled in retirement and endowment money two decades ago, have grown so large that their performance now resembles that of ordinary mutual funds.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Scale is eroding the alpha edge even in traditionally 'smart money' alternative asset classes, a red flag for allocators paying premium fees there.

insights.claim: A study of 715 S&P 500 additions and 711 deletions between January 1995 and June 2021 shows that both the positive price bump from being added and the negative bump from being removed have shrunk over time and largely disappeared in the last decade or two.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should not expect a mechanical pop for SpaceX, OpenAI or Anthropic if/when they're eventually added to the index.

insights.claim: This fading inclusion effect contradicts the intuitive expectation that as passive investing (and S&P 500-indexed assets) has grown, the benefit of being added to the index should have grown too — instead it has shrunk.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Undermines the 'passive flows create a guaranteed inclusion pop' thesis that some investors use to justify front-running index additions.

insights.claim: There is accumulating evidence that companies added to the S&P 500 are more likely to underperform than outperform in the twelve months following their addition.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Buying a stock purely on the news of upcoming S&P 500 inclusion has historically been a poor trade over a one-year horizon.
insights.quote: there is evidence accumulating that companies that get added to the S&P 500 are more likely to underperform than outperform in the twelve months after the addition

insights.claim: Tesla replaced Apartment Investment and Management (AIV) in the S&P 500 on December 18, 2020, and in the months afterward not only underperformed the S&P 500 itself but massively underperformed AIV, the company it replaced.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A concrete counter-example investors can cite against anyone arguing S&P 500 inclusion is automatically bullish for a stock.
insights.quote: Tesla not only under performed the S&P 500 in the months after its inclusion in the index, but massively underperformed the company (AIV) that it replaced in the index.

insights.claim: Damodaran predicts the price trajectories of SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic will not be materially altered by whether or when they're included in the S&P 500.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should value these companies on fundamentals/AI-story progress rather than positioning for an index-inclusion catalyst.

insights.claim: Damodaran is skeptical that passive investing is the cause of top-heavy markets, the disappearance of the small-cap premium, and momentum's dominance in the last decade, even though he concedes these trends are consistent with a 'passive feeds momentum' story.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Pushes back on a popular narrative used to justify restricting index funds; investors shouldn't assume passive flows alone explain mega-cap dominance.

insights.claim: Money flowing into index funds can also flow out, so any momentum effect from passive inflows can reverse in the other direction — and the fading S&P 500 inclusion price bump is itself evidence the market doesn't buy the momentum story.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A two-sided momentum mechanism means today's mega-cap winners face equal-and-opposite flow risk if passive allocations ever rotate away.

insights.claim: If momentum from passive flows explained large-cap dominance, valuation multiples (PE, EV/EBITDA) of large caps should be diverging upward from small caps across the board — instead, only a subset of large caps have won, and they've done so by capturing a disproportionate share of actual earnings growth, consistent with technology-driven winner-take-all economics rather than passive-flow mechanics.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Frames mega-cap outperformance as an earnings/disruption story (tie to his broader 'Mag Seven' winner-take-all thesis), not a passive-flow artifact — meaning fundamentals, not index mechanics, should drive stock selection among large caps.

insights.claim: Most active investing is built around publicly available information and a belief in mean reversion rather than genuine original research, and only a small subset of active investors and analysts actually make prices more informative.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Explains why the shrinking pool of active managers doesn't threaten market efficiency as much as doomsayers claim — most of that pool wasn't adding informational value anyway.

insights.claim: Fund managers warning that passive investing is destroying market efficiency are often the same people who previously dismissed efficient markets as an academic fantasy, making their current argument both hypocritical and self-serving; any pension or endowment manager steering money to active managers on that basis would violate fiduciary duty.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A sharp warning flag for allocators: 'protect market efficiency' pitches from active managers should be treated as self-interested marketing, not fiduciary-grade advice.
insights.quote: any endowment or pension fund manager who uses this argument to steer endowment funds to active money managers would be in violation of his or her fiduciary responsibility.

insights.claim: The argument that retail investors and retirees need protection from S&P 500 exposure to money-losing mega-caps is condescending, since an S&P 500 index-fund holder is far less exposed to any single stock's downside than an active manager who over- or under-weights that stock.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Diversified index exposure to a risky mega-cap is actually a lower-risk way for retail investors to get exposure than picking the stock directly or trusting an active manager's concentrated bet.

insights.claim: The notion that institutional 'smart money' can better time or price a company like SpaceX than retail investors is fanciful, because institutional investors behave more like traders than long-term investors, making them market followers rather than leaders.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Deflates the 'smart money will get it right, protect retail from itself' argument used against index inclusion of speculative mega-caps.
insights.quote: institutional investors are more traders than investors, making them market followers, not leader

insights.claim: The belief that profitable companies are inherently safe investments and money-losing companies are inherently risky ones is the weakest link in the anti-inclusion argument; a money-losing company can be a good investment at the right price, and a profitable, stable company can be a bad investment at the wrong price.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Reframes the SpaceX/OpenAI/Anthropic debate away from 'profitable vs unprofitable' toward valuation discipline — the real question is price paid, not current earnings status.
insights.quote: At the right price, a money-losing company can be a good investment and at the wrong price, a company with solid and stable profits can be a bad investment.

themes.theme: S&P's rule enforcement and the real power balance

insights.claim: Ahead of the SpaceX IPO, S&P announced it would keep its rule requiring a company to be listed and traded at least a year before index eligibility, meaning SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic will all have to wait a year even if they go public this year.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Sets a concrete timeline: index-inclusion catalysts for these three names are at least 12 months out from their respective IPO dates, regardless of market cap.

insights.claim: A year after listing, SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic will still be money-losing businesses with unproven business models and will remain corporate-governance horror stories — the one-year wait won't fix any of that.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors shouldn't expect the waiting period to resolve governance or profitability concerns before eventual index consideration.
insights.quote: a year after they list and start trading, these three companies will still be money losing businesses, with business models that are still works in progress and will remain corporate governance horror stories

insights.claim: S&P needs SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic in its index (to remain credible as a large-cap benchmark) more than these companies need to be in the index, so any future rule-bending will come from S&P's side, not the companies adapting to meet index requirements.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Bet on S&P eventually loosening or reinterpreting its own criteria for these companies rather than the companies changing behavior to qualify.
insights.quote: The bottom line is that S&P needs these companies in its index more than they need to be in the index, with the consequence that the companies will not go out of their way to meet index requirements that they feel are costly to them, and that if there is any bending, it will be S&P that does it.

insights.claim: The future price paths of SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic will be determined by how the AI story plays out on both substance (growth, unit economics, reinvestment) and perception (hype and momentum) — not by index membership.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Gives investors the explicit variables to track for these names: unit economics and reinvestment rates alongside sentiment/hype, rather than index-related catalysts.
